coredata.dc:title: Determination of chondroitin sulfate in synovial fluid and drug by ratiometric fluorescence strategy based on carbon dots quenched FAM-labeled ssDNA
coredata.dc:creator: Xiao, Jiecheng
Hao, Xiaoli
Miao, Chenfang
Li, Fenglan
Huang, Jianyong
Lin, Xinhua
Chen, Min
Wu, Xianwei
Weng, Shaohuang
coredata.dc:description: Chondroitin sulfate (CS) plays an increasingly important role in clinical settings and pharmacy quality control. However, sensitive and simple methods for CS detection remain limited. In this work, positively charged nitrogen doped carbon dots (P-NCDs) with internal luminescence and quenching property to FAM-labeled random-sequence ssDNA (F-ssDNA) were prepared by a simple heating method. P-NCDs attached and quenched F-ssDNA through electrostatic interaction to form the system of P-NCDs and F-ssDNA (P-NCDs/F-ssDNA) with retained fluorescence intensity of P-NCDs. The highly negatively charged CS reacted electrostatically with P-NCDs and then replaced F-ssDNA in P-NCDs/F-ssDNA to recover the fluorescence intensity of the original quenched F-ssDNA while retaining the internal fluorescence intensity of P-NCDs. Thus, by using restored F-ssDNA as the signal controlled by adding CS to P-NCDs/F-ssDNA, a ratiometric fluorescence strategy based on the retained fluorescence of P-NCDs as reference signal was fabricated through synchronous fluorescence spectrometry for the sensitive detection of CS. Under the optimal experimental conditions, a linear equation for CS was obtained for CS concentration within the range of 0.05–2.0 μg/mL. The method was also successfully applied for the accurate determination of CS in joint fluid samples of arthritic patients, chondroitin sulfate tablets, and chondroitin sulfate eye drops, suggesting its appreciable application potential in the clinic.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Positively charged nitrogen doped carbon dots (P-NCDs)
Synchronous fluorescence spectrometry
Ratiometric fluorescence strategy
Chondroitin sulfate
Synovial fluid

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization of P-NCDs and their interaction with F-ssDNA
Feasibility and detection mechanism of CS determination using P-NCDs/F-ssDNA
Analytical performance of the system of P-NCDs/F-ssDNA for CS detection
Real application of P-NCDs/F-ssDNA for CS detection from synthetic fluid of patients with arthritis and from drugs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the ratiometric fluorescence strategy based on P-NCDs integrated FAM-ssDNA for chondroitin sulfate detection.
Fluorescence spectra of (A) pure P-NCDs and F-ssDNA, and (B) P-NCDs, F-ssDNA and the the mixture of P-NCDs and F-ssDNA with the excitation at 340 nm or 488 nm.

Synchronous fluorescence spectrometry (A) and zeta potentials (B) of F-ssDNA, P-NCDs, P-NCDs/F-ssDNA with and without the addition CS. And the zeta potential of CS with and without the presence of P-NCDs (B). Error bar was the standard deviation of three parallel tests.

(A) Synchronous fluorescence spectrometry and (B) Relationship and linear relationship (inset) of the ratios of FF-ssDNA and FP-NCDs to the addition of variable CS into P-NCDs/F-ssDNA. The comparison of the ratios of FF-ssDNA and FP-NCDs to the 200 μg/mL different interferences and 6 μg/mL CS (C) and the 6 μg/mL CS with or without the presence of 200 μg/mL of variable interferences (D). Error bar was the standard deviation of three parallel tests.
The tested results and recovery of this method for CS in joint fluid samples.

row.entry: Tested CS in diluted joint fluid (μg/mL)

head.ce:title: Determination of chondroitin sulfate in synovial fluid and drug by ratiometric fluorescence strategy based on carbon dots quenched FAM-labeled ssDNA

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Chondroitin sulfate (CS) plays an increasingly important role in clinical settings and pharmacy quality control. However, sensitive and simple methods for CS detection remain limited. In this work, positively charged nitrogen doped carbon dots (P-NCDs) with internal luminescence and quenching property to FAM-labeled random-sequence ssDNA (F-ssDNA) were prepared by a simple heating method. P-NCDs attached and quenched F-ssDNA through electrostatic interaction to form the system of P-NCDs and F-ssDNA (P-NCDs/F-ssDNA) with retained fluorescence intensity of P-NCDs. The highly negatively charged CS reacted electrostatically with P-NCDs and then replaced F-ssDNA in P-NCDs/F-ssDNA to recover the fluorescence intensity of the original quenched F-ssDNA while retaining the internal fluorescence intensity of P-NCDs. Thus, by using restored F-ssDNA as the signal controlled by adding CS to P-NCDs/F-ssDNA, a ratiometric fluorescence strategy based on the retained fluorescence of P-NCDs as reference signal was fabricated through synchronous fluorescence spectrometry for the sensitive detection of CS. Under the optimal experimental conditions, a linear equation for CS was obtained for CS concentration within the range of 0.05–2.0 μg/mL. The method was also successfully applied for the accurate determination of CS in joint fluid samples of arthritic patients, chondroitin sulfate tablets, and chondroitin sulfate eye drops, suggesting its appreciable application potential in the clinic.

ce:keyword.ce:text: Positively charged nitrogen doped carbon dots (P-NCDs)

ce:section.ce:para: Chondroitin sulfate (CS) is an important glycosaminoglycan that primarily exists in the matrix of tissues, such as cartilage, ligaments, tendons, and arterial wall of human and animal [1]. It is an important symptomatic slow-acting drug used for treating osteoarthritis and is available as a prescription drug in Europe for the treatment of knee osteoarthritis [1 2]. Furthermore, CS is also applied as an important low-quality dietary supplement elsewhere to achieve osteoarthritis prevention and treatment [2 3]. In general, CS plays roles in the treatment or remission of osteoarthritis by improving joint space stenosis. CS also exhibits functions in the treatment of hyperlipidemia [4], hyperglycemia [5], and tumors [6]. In China, collagen CS calcium tablets, aminoglycoside, and calcium tablets have gradually become an indispensable health care product for people with osteoarthritis or low immunity. Moreover, CS with different sources and different extraction processes may exhibit large differences in quality, which increase the necessity in accurately determining CS content. As for the widespread application of CS, the quality control and monitoring of CS is an important and attractive task [7]. Thus, the pharmacokinetic study of polysaccharides in animals or humans have been widely studied [3,8], as well as convenient method for the detection of CS in vitro and in vivo.

Existing detection methods for CS primarily include high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [9,10], capillary electrophoresis (CE) [11,12], and spectroscopy [13–15]. Although HPLC and CE are rapid and high in accuracy and operability for CS detection, their complicated processes and potentially required enzymatic hydrolysis into CS disaccharide restrict their extensive application for CS detection [10,16]. Although CS can be determined through variable techniques, the operation of the spectroscopy method is cumbersome, and its reproducibility and specificity are relatively poor. The detection of complex drugs, like glycosaminoglycan, has been performed through fluorescence methods because of their simplicity, sensitivity, and selectivity [17–20]. Anionic aluminum phthalocyanine-cationic surfactant ion-association complex [21] and positive dendrimeric nanoparticle quenched graphene quantum dots [22] can be developed as fluorescent turn-on methods for CS detection. The abovementioned fluorescent methods for CS are primarily based on the modulation of the aggregation of the electrostatic interaction system controlled by negatively charged CS. However, the fluorescence signals of the reported works are centered at a single output, few works have focused on the potential design of a multi-response system for CS detection. Moreover, the possible need of developed fluorescent methods for some complex physiological environments, such as serum or synovial fluid, has considerable requirements.

Recently, ratiometric fluorescence strategy based on dual-mode signal has attracted considerable interests owing to its self-calibration function for potential diverse interferences from the instrument, temperature, and other environment factors, in contrast with a single-channel assay [23–25]. Furthermore, ratiometric routine can achieve more reliable quantitative determination for the quantification of the analyte. Therefore, several ratiometric methods have been fabricated for the sensitive detection of drugs [23,24], enzymes [25], biomarkers [26], and genes [27]. Although a ratiometric fluorescence method based on the introduction of fluorescent quantum dots has been developed [28,29], the use of ratiometric fluorescence strategy for other complex systems, such as synovial fluid, has been rarely explored. The detection of compositions in synovial fluid is an effective clinical diagnosis strategy for some bone and joint diseases [30,31]. Compared with other fluorescent nanomaterials for analytical strategy construction, carbon dots (CDs) have been a hot topic and have demonstrated various application in bioimaging [32–34], sensing [35–37] and photosciecne [38–40] ascribed to the biosafety, modulated optical properties and easy-control fabrication. Using the surface modulation or element doping or tunable synthetic strategies, specific carbon dots may be developed to ratiometric analytical methods [37,41] or biological application [42,43]. Hence, the reasonable application of some carbon dots can be applied to fabricate an effective assay for synovial fluid [31].

In this work, a simple but sensitive ratiometric homogeneous fluorescent platform based on the interaction of positively charged nitrogen doping carbon dots (P-NCDs) and FAM-labeled random-sequence ssDNA (F-ssDNA) was fabricated for CS detection via competitive electrostatic action. To realize the designed proposal, F-ssDNA was anchored on P-NCDs prepared from a simple melting routine on the basis of coexisting electrostatic action and weak nucleobase effect [44]. Furthermore, strong negatively charged CS exhibited competitive electrostatic action with P-NCDs compared with F-ssDNA. As shown in the detailed principle scheme in Fig. 1 , in the absence of CS, F-ssDNA electrostatically adsorbed on the surface of P-NCDs and quenched FAM, whereas the fluorescence emission from P-NCDs remained. The adsorbed F-ssDNA would be replaced by the introduction of CS due to the stronger electrostatic adhesion of CS on P-NCDs than F-ssDNA on P-NCDs accompanied with the recovered fluorescence of F-ssDNA. P-NCDs retained its stable fluorescence in the presence of CS. As a consequence, the increased fluorescence of F-ssDNA (FFAM) and stable P-NCDs (FP-NCDs) would induce the enhancement of FFAM/FP-NCDs values for CS quantification. This work takes advantage of the combination of both competitive electrostatic interactions from different substrates and ratiometric dual-signal routine to achieve CS detection with sensitivity, simplicity, and reproducibility. Thus, the method can accurately detect CS in synovial fluid and determine CS content in drugs, suggesting its potential power for clinical diagnosis and medicine quality control.
Materials and Apparatus were illustrated in supplemental materials.

Approximately 1.0 g of hydrated citric acid and 0.3 g of l-glutathione was fully mixed in a 25 mL round bottom flask. The mixture was heated at 140 °C in oil bath for 30 min to obtain a melted liquid. After the heating temperature rose from 140 °C to 180 °C, 5 mL of polyethene polyamine was added into the melted liquid. After continuous heating for 1 h at 180 °C, the mixture was cooled to room temperature. After the addition of acetone, the reaction was centrifugally separated to obtain a brown precipitate. The precipitate was purified through dialysis. After freeze-drying, P-NCDs were obtained and stored at 4 °C in the dark.

P-NCDs and F-ssDNA were diluted with PBS (10 mM, pH 7.4). In a 0.2 mL total reacting solution, 50 nM F-ssDNA was mixed with 2.5 μg/mL P-NCDs. Various concentrations of CS were then added into the system of P-NCDs and F-ssDNA (P-NCDs/F-ssDNA). Considering the fluorescence property of P-NCDs and F-ssDNA, the fluorescence spectra of the detecting solution were obtained using synchronous fluorescence spectrometry [45]; this technique can monitor the simultaneous fluorescence changes of the dual fluorescence behaviors of P-NCDs and F-ssDNA in the detection system. Δλ of the survey of synchronous fluorescence spectrometry was set to 20 nm (Δλ= λem- λex) to record the fluorescence intensities.

The testing procedure for CS was carried out in accordance with Section “CS determination”.
i)
Detection of CS content and the recovery of CS in joint fluid

The extraction of joint fluid from patients with arthritis was approved by the Ethics Committee of Affiliated Hospital of Putian University. All of the patients had signed informed consent. Joint fluid was centrifuged at 3000 r/min for 20 min at 4 °C, and the supernatant was collected for further detection. The supernatant of joint fluid was diluted with PBS (10 mM, pH 7.4) to 1%. After CS content determination in the diluted joint fluid, different concentrations of CS were added into diluted joint fluid, and the recovery was tested.
ii)
Testing of the percentage labeled content of CS in compound CS tablet and CS eye drops

Ten tablets of CS were weighed and crushed. Depending on the average weight of the tablet, the powder was weighed to approximately the mass of one tablet. The powder was then dissolved, filtered, and diluted with PBS (10 mM, pH 7.4) for synchronous fluorescence testing. For the CS eye drops, a certain volume of the drop was diluted directly to a certain concentration with PBS (10 mM, pH 7.4) for synchronous fluorescence testing. The recorded fluorescence intensity was employed to calculate the percentage labeled content of CS in CS tablet and CS eye drops.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of P-NCDs and their interaction with F-ssDNA
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology and optical property of P-NCDs were characterized. As shown in Fig. S1, P-NCDs exhibited relatively uniform and monodisperse spherical particles with an average diameter of 2.2 nm. The lattice spacing obtained from HRTEM was 0.22 nm, which is in accordance with the graphite carbon facet of (100) [46]. FTIR was introduced to evaluate the molecular structure of P-NCDs, as shown in Fig. S2. The characteristic absorption bands at 1654, 1555, and 1125 cm−1 illustrate the respective presence of stretching vibration of CO, bending vibration of NH, and asymmetric stretching vibration of CNC, suggesting the main elemental compositions of C, N, and O in P–NCDs with the doping effect of N. Moreover, the presence of the stretching vibrations of −OH and –NH2 was evidenced by the wide peaks at 3440 and 3277 cm−1. Moreover, P-NCDs exhibited good dispersion in water with the positively charged surface, as shown by the tested zeta-potential of 12.47 mV. P-NCDs also exhibited specific and strong fluorescence maximum emission at 420 nm under the suitable excitation at 340 nm (Fig. 2 A). They illustrated a wavelength dependent property when excited from 340 nm to 390 nm (Fig. S3). The quantum yield of P-NCDs was calculated to be 9.5 % by using quinine sulfate (54 %) as the standard reference. F-ssDNA showed specific and strong FAM fluorescence emission at 520 nm under excitation at 488 nm (Fig. 2A). Considering the contrary charge of positive P-NCDs and the known negatively charged phosphate backbone of F-ssDNA, the possibility of interaction between P-NCDs and F-ssDNA was further investigated. The different fluorescence wavelengths of P-NCDs and FAM may obtain variable luminescent properties of the P-NCDs and F-ssDNA mixture in ordinary condition. In the presence or absence of F-ssDNA with P-NCDs, the fluorescence behavior of P-NCDs was stable with an emission wavelength of 420 nm. However, the fluorescence of original F-ssDNA centered at 520 nm was quenched more than 90 %, with the coexistence of P-NCDs (Fig. 2B). The variable F-ssDNA with or without P-NCDs illustrated the interaction of the system in P-NCDs/F-ssDNA with the maintenance of the fluorescence property of P-NCDs, suggesting the sensing potential of P-NCDs/F-ssDNA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Feasibility and detection mechanism of CS determination using P-NCDs/F-ssDNA
ce:section.ce:para: Considering the different and independent excitation and emission wavelengths of P-NCDs and F-ssDNA, synchronous fluorescence spectrometry with constant Δλ [45] was applied to monitor the variable fluorescence process of P-NCDs/F-ssDNA simultaneously. As shown in Fig. 3 A, P-NCDs and F-ssDNA showed independent fluorescence emission at 360 nm and 490 nm, respectively. For P-NCDs/F-ssDNA, the fluorescence emission at 490 nm attributed to F-ssDNA was quenched, whereas P-NCDs retained its stable emission. CS addition can restore the quenched F-ssDNA at 490 nm while maintaining the fluorescence intensity of P-NCDs. These results revealed the quenching effect of P-NCDs on F-ssDNA and confirmed the feasibility of CS detection by using P-NCDs/F-ssDNA considering the increased intensity of F-ssDNA. Moreover, the stable fluorescence emission of P-NCDs can serve as an internal reference for the variable fluorescence intensity of F-ssDNA controlled by CS. This sensing platform can be developed as a ratiometric strategy. To the best of our knowledge, this approach is the first example of CS monitoring by a ratiometric strategy by using the simple mixing operation of P-NCDs and F-ssDNA.

The detailed testing mechanism was investigated. As shown in Fig. 3B, the interaction process of CS introduction on P-NCDs/F-ssDNA was further verified by zeta potential measurement. P-NCDs changed the zeta-potential from 12.47 mV to 2.0 mV due to the addition of negative F-ssDNA (-7.2 mV) to form P-NCDs/F-ssDNA. The decreased zeta potential suggests electrostatic interaction between negatively charged F-ssDNA and P-NCDs. When strong negatively charged CS (-12.5 mV) was introduced into P-NCDs/F-ssDNA, the mixture showed decreased zeta-potential to -19.75 mV, which was much lower than that of P-NCDs/F-ssDNA. Combined with the zeta-potential of the mixture of P-NCDs and CS (-4.5 mV), these results reveal that electrostatic interaction between P-NCDs and CS occurred and be stronger than the electrostatic interaction in P-NCDs/F-ssDNA. To further understand the interaction, we applied fluorescence anisotropic measurement to monitor changes in the fluorescence anisotropic value (r) of F-ssDNA during the testing process by using P-NCDs/F-ssDNA. The variable r represents the interaction of fluorophore-labeled DNA and nanomaterial; an increased r suggests an increased absorption interaction between DNA and nanomaterials, and then the fluorophore-labeled DNA was quenched [47]. As shown in Fig. S3C, r of free F-ssDNA was 2.31, whereas the fluorescence anisotropy of FAM in P-NCDs/F-ssDNA was higher (r = 8.64), suggesting the strong attachment of F-ssDNA on P-NCDs in P-NCDs/F-ssDNA [47,48]. Moreover, CS addition into P-NCDs/F-ssDNA decreased r to 2.25, which was close to that of free F-ssDNA. This result suggests the detachment of F-ssDNA from P-NCDs in P-NCDs/F-ssDNA by CS. Combined with the electrostatic interaction in P-NCDs/F-ssDNA, fluorescence anisotropic results prove the detecting mechanism that CS destroys the electrostatic interaction between P-NCDs and F-ssDNA in P-NCDs/F-ssDNA, then CS replaces F-ssDNA and adsorbs onto P-NCDs due to its strongly negative charge.

Several conditions were optimized to improve the sensitivity of the ratiometric method for CS detection. First, the effect of P-NCDs content to F-ssDNA quenching was studied. As shown in Fig. S5A, increasing P-NCDs promotes the quenching degree, and the quenching plateau is reached when P-NCDs are higher than 2.5 μg/mL. Moreover, the inset in Fig. S5B shows that the fluorescence intensity of F-ssDNA decreased rapidly after P-NCDs were added and reached equilibrium minimum within 1 min. This result indicates the rapid adsorption and quenching of F-ssDNA by P-NCDs. Accordingly, the release of F-ssDNA fluorescence by CS introduction was relatively slow, and an equilibrium stable value was reached within 20 min (Fig. S5B). Therefore, 20 min was selected as the appropriate reaction time for CS detection with 2.5 μg/mL P-NCDs. The possible effect of temperature to the testing system was further investigated, as shown in Fig. S6A. Temperature had minimal effect on the recovered fluorescence for CS detection. Thus room temperature is the appropriate condition for this strategy, given the enhanced operability. The effect of pH on the testing performance of the proposed method was also investigated. As shown in Fig. S6B, F-ssDNA showed increased intensity with increased pH due to the pH-sensitive FAM.P-NCDs/F-ssDNA retained its stable fluorescence intensity at pH of < 7.4. The fluorescence intensity was increased at pH > 8.0 due to the pH-sensitive FAM. Moreover, addition CS enhanced the fluorescence intensity of P-NCDs/F-ssDNA with gradually increased pH and achieved high response at pH 7.4 and 8.0. Considering the human physiological state and the pH-sensitive FAM, pH 7.4 was considered as the optimum condition for this testing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of the system of P-NCDs/F-ssDNA for CS detection
ce:section.ce:para: The response of P-NCDs/F-ssDNA for different contents of CS was evaluated. Under the optimal conditions, with increased CS concentration, the fluorescence of P-NCDs remained stable; however, the fluorescence response of F-ssDNA centered at 490 nm gradually increased (Fig. 4 A). With the stable fluorescence of P-NCDs, the ratio between the varied fluorescence at 490 nm and the stable fluorescence at 360 nm could be obtained and applied to monitor the amount of CS, as shown in Fig. 4B. The ratios (FF-ssDNA/FP-NCDs) increased regularly with increased CS and reached the plateau attributed to the capacity of P-NCDs/F-ssDNA. In the range of 0.05–2 μg/mL, CS concentration was in a linear relationship with FF-ssDNA/FP-NCDs according to FF-ssDNA / FP-NCDs = 0.09674 + 0.51226 CCS(μg/mL) (R2 = 0.99247). The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 6.83 ng/mL based on 3σ/slope (n = 9). The reproducibility of this method was assessed by six independent testing for CS detection. The relative standard deviation (RSD) values of the tests were 1.5 % and 1.7 % for 0.5 μg/mL and 1 μg/mL of CS, respectively, suggesting the excellent reliability of the constructed fluorescent strategy. Compared with previous strategies for CS, this ratiometric assay based on P-NCDs/F-ssDNA exhibited better or comparable analytical performance for CS, especially given its low LOD (Table S1). Furthermore, this strategy displayed convenient operation, free surface modification, and improved reproducibility ascribed to the self-calibration function of ratiometric strategy.

Selectivity is an important factor for CS detection in practical applications on biological samples. Fig. 4C exhibits the change in fluorescence intensity of the addition of common interfering substances into P-NCDs/F-ssDNA. Although the high concentration HA had a little recovery signal on the detection system, the response was less than 1/4 response of that caused by CS. And the interference of other substances on P-NCDs/F-ssDNA was negligible. Furthermore, the possible interference of common substances on the testing process of CS using P-NCDs/F-ssDNA was also investigated (Fig. 4D). The coexisting of high concentration of NaCl in CS affected about 1/3 testing response of pure CS in P-NCDs/F-ssDNA. The phenomenon was due to that high content of NaCl may increase salt concentration and thus affect the electrostatic interaction in the system of CS, P-NCDs, and F-ssDNA [44]. Except for NaCl, all other substances had ignorable effects on the detection response of CS using P-NCDs/F-ssDNA. Considering the low content of NaCl in the synthetic fluid or drugs containing CS, these findings confirmed the negligible interference of common substance and some analogs in accurate CS detection using this method, suggesting the high selectivity of this analytical method for CS.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Real application of P-NCDs/F-ssDNA for CS detection from synthetic fluid of patients with arthritis and from drugs
ce:section.ce:para: For the real application of this method for CS, in the same testing process, changes in the ratio (FF-ssDNA/FP-NCDs) before and after the addition of treated samples were measured. CS content was calculated from the obtained ratio by using the linear equation shown in Fig. 4B. First, the content of CS in several joint fluid samples from patients with arthritis was measured to check the feasibility of this method in clinical diagnosis. As shown in Table 1 , different joint fluid samples contained variable concentrations of CS. The recoveries of different CS additions in three joint fluid samples occurred within the range of 96.0 %–101.9 % with RSDs less than 3.30 %, suggesting the excellent accuracy and reproducibility of the proposed ratiometric strategy using P-NCDs/F-ssDNA.

Moreover, Compound Chondroitin Sulfate Tablet and Chondroitin Sulfate Eye Drop were employed to investigate the application of this method in pharmaceutical analysis through CS content determination. The treatment of the tablets and drops was performed in accordance with the prescribed procedure of the Chinese Pharmacopoeia (2015). The percentage of labeled quantity of CS tablets and CS eye drops were tested to be 98.2 % and 101.4 %, respectively. The results satisfy the demands of Chinese Pharmacopoeia (2015) for content, suggesting the application potential of this method for the accurate detection of drugs containing CS. The detection results of CS in real synovial fluids and drugs reflect that the developed ratiometric fluorescence method is an excellent potential tool for clinical medicine.

We reported a simple, effective ratiometric fluorescent strategy for CS detection based on P-NCDs/F-ssDNA. The full advantage of the positive P-NCDs and negative F-ssDNA was considered and used to establish effective competition of CS for F-ssDNA on P-NCDs considering the diverse electrostatic interaction strengths in P-NCDs/F-ssDNA. The detection can be carried out rapidly at room temperature without any catalyst or oxidant. Under the optimal conditions, the CS detection method showed good linear response with low detection limit. The proposed method, which had the advantages of efficiency, convenience, and high sensitivity, was successfully applied to detect CS in actual joint fluid, showing its potential clinical applications. Overall, this work provided new insights into the fabrication of interacting fluorescent nanomaterials and DNA based on various interaction forms or strengths for biosensing.

Jiecheng Xiao: Conceptualization, Methodology, Data curation, Formal analysis, Visualization, Writing - original draft, Project administration, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Writing - review & editing. Xiaoli Hao: Data curation, Formal analysis, Visualization, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing. Chenfang Miao: Data curation, Formal analysis, Visualization, Writing - original draft. Fenglan Li: Investigation. Jianyong Huang: Conceptualization, Methodology. Xinhua Lin: Resources. Min Chen: Project administration, Supervision, Funding acquisition. Xianwei Wu: Conceptualization, Methodology. Shaohuang Weng: Project administration, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors have declared that no competing interest exists.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge for financial support from the Joint Funds for the innovation of science and Technology, Fujian province (2017Y9121, 2017Y9124), the National Science Foundation of Fujian Province (2017Y0042, 2017J01328, 2019J01584), the Elite Cultivation Program of Health and Family Planning of Fujian Province (2017-ZQN-61) and Medical Innovation Program of Fujian Province (2018-CXB-8).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.colsurfb.2020.111030.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
